title: China GP Reactions: Cashinho Penalty Overshadows Ferrari's Perfect Start
meta: Published: 16 March 2026 | By Olly Bradshaw, Paddock Insider | F1CC News
caption: The Ferrari garage — a complicated picture beneath the celebration.
body:
Eetu Väisänen won. Again. Ferrari lead both championships. Two from two.

And yet.

The conversation in the Shanghai paddock after the chequered flag wasn’t about the winner. It was about the Ferrari driver who finished 15th and collected his sixth penalty point of a season that is only two races old.

Sources close to the team describe the post‑race atmosphere as “celebratory, but with an edge.”

That edge has a name.

Afonso Cachinho.

Cachinho’s Late Penalty — and the Six‑Point Warning

In the closing stages, with the result long since decided, Cachinho put a move on Riley Swansson that was never on. Stewards called it “forceful and poorly‑judged.” Five seconds added. That’s six penalty points across two weekends, plus a formal warning.

Six points in two rounds.

At that rate, does he make it to the summer without a race ban? The maths is uncomfortable.

Paddock observers noted that Cachinho declined to comment extensively afterwards. His engineers are understood to be working on his racecraft under pressure. The charitable read: a driver overreaching in a compromised car. The less charitable read is already forming…

Belacik: The Seat Conversation Continues

Randy Belacik gave another carefully worded response. Two Q1 exits. Zero points. Now a penalty for avoidable contact.

These are not results that can be packaged as an “adaptation period.”

Belacik has not publicly named a replacement. But the message from Maranello is unmistakable: the window is very, very short.

One source close to the Ferrari driver programme suggested that “certain names in F2 are being watched more closely than they were a fortnight ago.”

Interesting.

Roy’s Anger at the Pit Wall

Jason Roy’s post‑race manner was noticeably terse. Pole position. Led the opening phase. Then watched the race slip away through a pit call he did not endorse.

He held a lengthy debrief with Red Bull’s engineering group on Sunday evening. The pit call under the early safety car — switch to hards — was the central point of contention.

Red Bull’s defence? The free tyre window was too good to pass up.

Roy’s position, expressed bluntly by all accounts? That the team handed Ferrari the race on a plate.

Does that disagreement linger into Suzuka…?

Keisner: The Story Nobody Expected

If Roy left Shanghai frustrated, Robin Keisner left it with a growing recognition that might be the most unexpected development of the young season.

A sprint podium on Saturday. A P5 recovery from last on the grid on Sunday. Driver of the Day.

The Audi driver said simply: “We knew the car could do more than people expected. We are doing more.”

That understated confidence, combined with actual results, is exactly how momentum builds in a long championship.

Quietly, Audi now have 18 points from two weekends. That’s more than McLaren. More than Aston Martin.

Väisänen: Two from Two, But No Complacency

The championship leader kept it brief. Two wins from two is not a position that requires explaining in front of cameras — it requires protecting.

He noted that Japan will present a completely different challenge: higher tyre loading, a track that exposes aerodynamic efficiency more honestly.

“We have a quick car. We have to keep it that way.”

Championship Picture After Two Rounds

Väisänen leads. Kevu sits second. Ferrari lead the constructors’.

But underneath, it’s genuinely complex. Racing Bulls are second in the constructors’. Keisner’s Audi is punching above its weight. Springfield’s Aston Martin continues to surprise.

Suzuka will be the first test on a track with enough high‑speed load to expose where the real performance margin lies.

And it might also tell us whether Afonso Cachinho is still a Ferrari driver in anything but name.

Watch this space.
